perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall be for one (1) year commencing on the Effective Date and automatically renewing annually thereafter, unless either party provides a thirty-day notice of written termination one to the other ("Term").
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall be for one (1) year commencing on the Effective Date and automatically renewing annually thereafter, unless either party provides a ten-day notice of written termination one to the other ("Term").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to ten days may conflict with state laws requiring longer notice periods for contract termination, especially if the agreement is interpreted under broader commercial or franchise regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL 445.1527(1) generally requires a 60-day notice period for termination or cancellation of a franchise agreement.  Although this agreement is not explicitly defined as a franchise, if the relationship between ESSI and Talent were deemed a franchise relationship under Michigan law, the ten-day notice period would be in direct violation of this statute. The original 30 day notice is more legally sound.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1527 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1527 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 269 of 1974 Previous Section Next Section FRANCHISE INVESTMENT LAW (EXCERPT) Act 269 of 1974 445.1527 Void and unenforceable provisions. Sec. 27. Each of the following provisions is void and unenforceable if contained in any documents relating to a franchise: (a) A prohibition on the right of a franchisee to join an association of franchisees. (b) A requirement that a franchisee assent to a release, assignment, novation, waiver, or estoppel which deprives a franchisee of rights and protections provided in this act. This shall not preclude a franchisee, after entering into a franchise agreement, from settling any and all claims. (c) A provision that permits a franchisor to terminate a franchise prior to the expiration of its term except for good cause. Good cause shall include the failure of the franchisee to comply with any lawful provision of the franchise agreement and to cure such failure after being given written notice thereof and a reasonable opportunity, which in no event need be more than 30 days, to cure such failure. (d) A provision that permits a franchisor to refuse to renew a franchise without fairly compensating the franchisee by repurchase or other means for the fair market value at the time of expiration of the franchisee's inventory, supplies, equipment, fixtures, and furnishings. Personalized materials which have no value to the franchisor and inventory, supplies, equipment, fixtures, and furnishings not reasonably required in the conduct of the franchise business are not subject to compensation. This subsection applies only if: (i) The term of the franchise is less than 5 years and (ii) the franchisee is prohibited by the franchise or other agreement from continuing to conduct substantially the same business under another trademark, service mark, trade name, logotype, advertising, or other commercial symbol in the same area subsequent to the expiration of the franchise
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation posits that if the agreement were deemed a franchise under Michigan law, the 10-day notice would violate MCL 445.1527(1), which generally requires a 60-day notice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically MCL 445.1527(c), states that good cause for termination must include a reasonable opportunity to cure failure, which need not be more than 30 days. Although this refers to 'good cause' termination, the Law Explanation suggests a broader application to franchise agreements, indicating that reducing the notice period to 10 days creates a legal contradiction if the agreement falls under franchise regulations.

perturbation.original_text: ESSI will provide Talent with one-million (1,000,000) shares of restricted common stock issued within ten business days of execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: ESSI will provide Talent with one-million (1,000,000) shares of restricted common stock issued within ninety business days of execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the issuance of stock could violate securities laws regarding timely delivery of securities, especially if the delay isn't properly disclosed or justified.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 15c6-1 mandates that the standard settlement cycle for most broker-dealer transactions be two business days. While this rule primarily targets broker-dealers, a 90-day delay could be interpreted as creating undue settlement delays if the stock is intended to be freely tradable shortly after issuance, potentially leading to scrutiny regarding compliance with prompt and accurate clearance and settlement procedures. The original text of ten days is more in line with market norms.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.15c6-1 - Settlement cycle. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.15c6-1 - Settlement cycle. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.15c6-1 Settlement cycle. (a) Except as provided in paragraphs (b), (c), and (d) of this section , a broker or dealer shall not effect or enter into a contract for the purchase or sale of a security (other than an exempted security , a government security , a municipal security , commercial paper, bankers' acceptances, or commercial bills) that provides for payment of funds and delivery of securities later than the first business day after the date of the contract unless otherwise expressly agreed to by the parties at the time of the transaction. (b) Paragraph (a) of this section shall not apply to: (1) Contracts for the purchase or sale of limited partnership interests that are not listed on an exchange or for which quotations are not disseminated through an automated quotation system of a registered securities association; (2) Security -based swaps; or (3) Contracts for the purchase or sale of securities that the Commission may from time to time, taking into account then existing market practices, exempt by order from the requirements of paragraph (a) of this section , either unconditionally or on specified terms and conditions , if the Commission determines that such exemption is consistent with the public interest and the protection of investors. (c) Paragraph (a) of this section shall not apply to contracts for the sale for cash of securities that are priced after 4:30 p.m. Eastern Time (ET) on the date such securities are priced and that are sold by an issuer to an underwriter pursuant to a firm commitment underwritten offering registered under the Securities Act of 1933 or sold to an initial purchaser by a broker-dealer participating in such offering provided that a broker or dealer shall not effect
perturbation.justification: The modification from 10 to 90 business days for issuing restricted common stock creates a contradiction with SEC Rule 15c6-1 regarding timely settlement, as explained in the Law Explanation. Snippet 2 states that 'a broker or dealer shall not effect or enter into a contract for the purchase or sale of a security...that provides for payment of funds and delivery of securities later than the first business day after the date of the contract'. While this rule directly applies to broker-dealers, the significant delay introduced by the changed text (90 days versus the original 10) creates inconsistency with the principle of prompt settlement. This is a change to a deadline, and falls under the definition of inconsistencies legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Said written approval must be given within five (5) business days of Talent's receipt or said usage shall be deemed unapproved.
perturbation.changed_text: Said written approval must be given within two (2) business days of Talent's receipt or said usage shall be deemed unapproved.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the approval timeframe to two days may be unreasonably short, potentially infringing on Talent's rights to proper review and approval, which could be viewed as an unfair or unconscionable business practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL 445.903(1)(e) prohibits unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in trade or commerce. If a two-day review period is deemed insufficient for Talent to reasonably assess ESSI's usage of Talent Attributes, especially concerning dietary supplements and related disclaimers, it could be argued as an unfair or unconscionable practice, particularly if Talent lacks specialized knowledge to make informed decisions within such a limited timeframe. The original text of five days is more legally sound.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 331 of 1976 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN CONSUMER PROTECTION ACT (EXCERPT) Act 331 of 1976 445.903 Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in conduct of trade or commerce; rules; applicability of subsection (1)(hh). Sec. 3. (1) Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce are unlawful and are defined as follows: (a) Causing a probability of confusion or misunderstanding as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (b) Using deceptive representations or deceptive designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (c) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that he or she does not have. (d) Representing that goods are new if they are deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, used, or secondhand. (e) Representing that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, or grade, or that goods are of a particular style or model, if they are of another. (f) Disparaging the goods, services, business, or reputation of another by false or misleading representation of fact. (g) Advertising or representing goods or services with intent not to dispose of those goods or services as advertised or represented. (h) Advertising goods or services with intent not to supply reasonably expectable public demand, unless the advertisement discloses a limitation of quantity in immediate conjunction with the advertised goods or services. (i) Making false or misleading statements of fact concerning the reasons for, existence of, or amounts of price reductions. (j) Representing that a part, replacement, or repair service is needed when it is not. (k) Representing to a party to whom goods
perturbation.justification: The change from five business days to two for Talent's approval creates a contradiction under the Michigan Consumer Protection Act, specifically MCL 445.903(1)(e), which prohibits unfair or unconscionable practices. As the Law Explanation states, a two-day review period might be insufficient for Talent to reasonably assess ESSI's usage, particularly regarding dietary supplements. The original five-day period is more legally sound. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that "Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce are unlawful". The reduced timeframe could be deemed an unfair practice, making the change a contradiction.
